food_subgroup: Beef — grilled or pan-seared steak cuts

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated strip loin steak entry; A0CKL (beef loin, grilled) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226848000 covers beef steak generically; no strip-loin-specific code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of strip loin steak is approximately 200 g (roughly 7 oz), representing a single steak portion as commonly plated in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, loin, top loin steak, boneless, separable lean only, trimmed to 1/8" fat, all grades, cooked, grilled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #23527
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked, grilled beef top loin steak (strip loin), lean only, trimmed to 1/8-inch fat, all grades. This is the canonical USDA entry for strip loin steak as served. Values represent the cooked, grilled state consistent with restaurant preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition / University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure beef (no carbohydrate) has an effectively zero GI per the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI tables. As beef contains no digestible carbohydrate and therefore does not raise blood glucose, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) as per Veritas v2 convention for carbohydrate-free foods; gi_category is low by convention. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 because carbohydrate_g per serving is 0.

hypertension.guidance: Strip loin steak contains approximately 64 mg sodium per 100 g in its plain grilled form, which is low; however, individuals with hypertension should avoid high-sodium marinades, sauces, or seasoning blends that may be added during restaurant preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate content and high protein
type2_diabetes.guidance: Strip loin steak contains no digestible carbohydrate and has a glycemic index of 1 (effectively zero), making it a suitable protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions and without high-carbohydrate accompaniments or sugary sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Strip loin steak provides approximately 3.4 g saturated fat and 90 mg cholesterol per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 100–150 g and choose lean trimmed cuts to manage saturated fat intake in line with AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Strip loin steak, also known as New York strip or top loin steak, is a premium beef cut taken from the short loin section of the cattle, located along the upper back behind the ribs. It is characterised by a firm texture, moderate marbling, and a robust, beefy flavour with a slightly chewy bite compared to more tender cuts such as tenderloin. The cut is typically boneless and trimmed of excess fat, leaving a thin fat cap on one edge that renders during cooking and contributes to flavour. In restaurant settings, strip loin steak is most commonly grilled over high heat or pan-seared in a cast-iron skillet to develop a caramelised crust (Maillard reaction) while retaining a juicy interior. Doneness levels range from rare to well-done, with medium-rare (internal temperature 57–60°C) considered optimal for flavour and texture. Per 100 g of cooked lean strip loin steak, the food provides approximately 207 kcal, 30.5 g protein, 9.0 g fat (3.4 g saturated), and 0 g carbohydrate. It is an excellent source of complete protein, zinc (5.0 mg), vitamin B12 (1.8 mcg), niacin (8.5 mg), and selenium (33 mcg). The absence of carbohydrate means it has a glycemic index of 1 (effectively zero) and does not raise blood glucose, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic dietary patterns. Clinically, the saturated fat content warrants moderation for individuals with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia. In Singapore, strip loin steak appears on menus at Western and fusion restaurants, including Mexican-themed establishments that incorporate grilled beef dishes. It is served as a main course, often accompanied by sides such as fries, salad, or grilled vegetables.

How to cook strip loin steak?
Is strip loin steak suitable for a low-carb diet?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked, grilled beef top loin steak (strip loin), lean only — an exact match to the food as served. GI of 1 (effectively zero) is well-established for carbohydrate-free beef in peer-reviewed GI literature. Ontology codes are defensible and documented.
notes: carbohydrate_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; beef strip loin contains no digestible carbohydrate by nature — naturally zero. sugar_g is naturally zero as beef contains no sugars. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as beef is an animal product containing no dietary fibre. vitamin_c_mg is confirmed zero per USDA source; beef contains negligible vitamin C after cooking — confirmed zero. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is confirmed zero per USDA source for lean cooked beef strip loin — confirmed zero. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for this specific cut. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as beef contains no digestible carbohydrate; gi_category is low by convention; glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate content is zero. The input record listed cuisine as Mexican and food_group as Mixed; cuisine has been corrected to Western as strip loin steak is a Western beef cut, and food_group corrected to Meat and alternatives. The field survey data listing at two Mexican-themed restaurants reflects the serving context but does not alter the food's canonical identity. Halal status is marked false as it cannot be confirmed from the input data; individual restaurant certification should be verified. Price data (SGD 38.00) preserved from input for reference only and not included in the nutritional record.
